flashcards.Back: The process of carrying traffic from multiple VLANs over a single physical link between two switches or a switch and a router, using tagging to identify the VLAN ID of each frame.
The industry-standard trunking protocol that inserts a 4-byte VLAN tag into the original Ethernet frame header. it is supported by almost all modern networking vendors.
A legacy Cisco-proprietary trunking protocol that encapsulates the entire Ethernet frame. it is now largely obsolete and replaced by the more efficient 802.1Q standard.
A switch interface configured to support multiple VLANs simultaneously. it uses encapsulation to ensure frames are delivered to the correct logical network on the receiving end.
The specific VLAN ID on an 802.1Q trunk that carries all untagged traffic. For security, both ends of a trunk must share the same native VLAN configuration.
The action of adding a unique identifier to a frame's header as it enters a trunk link, allowing switches to distinguish between traffic from different broadcast domains.
A Cisco-proprietary protocol used to automatically negotiate whether a link should function as a trunk or an access port between two directly connected switches.
The configured state of a port's trunking negotiation (e.g., dynamic auto, dynamic desirable, or static trunk), which defines how it interacts with its neighbor.
The actual resulting trunking state of an interface (either 'trunk' or 'static access') after DTP negotiation with the connected neighbor is complete.
A DTP mode where the port actively initiates trunking negotiation by sending DTP frames. it will form a trunk if the neighbor is 'auto', 'desirable', or 'on'.
A DTP mode where the port waits passively for the neighbor to initiate negotiation. it will only trunk if the neighbor is set to 'desirable' or 'on'.
The command that statically forces a switch port into trunking mode. it will attempt to negotiate a trunk link even if the neighbor is in 'dynamic auto' mode.
A security command that disables DTP on an interface. it is used when a trunk is manually configured to prevent the switch from sending discovery frames.
A configuration on a trunk port that restricts which VLANs are permitted to cross the link. by default, all VLANs (1–4094) are allowed.
A malicious attack where a device sends frames with multiple VLAN tags to gain unauthorized access to a different VLAN, often exploiting misconfigured native VLANs.
The primary Cisco command used to verify the operational status of trunk links, displaying encapsulation, native VLAN, and the list of allowed VLANs.
A detailed command that provides the administrative and operational trunking parameters for an interface, including DTP mode and native VLAN settings.
The process of removing unnecessary VLAN traffic from a trunk link to save bandwidth. it ensures that broadcast frames are only sent to switches that have active ports in that VLAN.
The specific framing method used on a trunk link. modern switches use 'dot1q' (802.1Q), while older switches might support 'isl' or 'negotiate'.
The 4-byte field inserted into an Ethernet header by 802.1Q. it includes the Priority field, the Canonical Format Identifier, and the 12-bit VLAN ID.
A configuration error where two ends of a trunk link have different native VLAN IDs, which can lead to traffic leaking between different VLANs.
The Cisco IOS command used to change the native VLAN ID for an 802.1Q trunk from its default value of 1.
The specific command syntax used to add one or more VLANs to the existing allowed list on a trunk port without overwriting the previous configuration.
VLAN Trunking Protocol. while its name implies it handles trunking, it actually synchronizes the VLAN database (IDs and names) across switches in a domain.
A specific type of VLAN hopping attack where an attacker adds two 802.1Q tags to a frame. The first tag is stripped by the first switch, and the second tag allows it to enter a different VLAN.
The user-configured intent for an interface, which can be 'on' (trunk), 'off' (access), 'desirable', or 'auto'.
The actual real-time status of an interface, indicating whether it is currently functioning as a trunk or an access port.
A legacy inter-VLAN routing method where a single trunk link connects a switch to a router, with the router interface divided into multiple logical subinterfaces.
A logical division of a physical router interface, each assigned a unique VLAN ID and IP address to perform routing between different broadcast domains.
The behavior where a switch receives a frame without an 802.1Q tag on a trunk port and forwards it to the native VLAN broadcast domain.
